analysis:
- Common pipeline: fast nondominated filtering → cheap per-point proxy ranking (box-volume and few-weight scalarizations) → build k-proportional shortlist → CELF-style lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs (sometimes pairwise caches) → bounded 1-for-1 local swaps → deterministic padding. Stronger heuristics tune shortlist size, scalarization density, and swap budget; weaker ones either overdo HV calls or rely on heavier weight/vector algebra without careful HV reuse.
- Hypervolume calls are the dominant cost. Best examples minimize calls via: singleton caching, selective pairwise caching only for small shortlists, lazy (timestamped) CELF updates, and small deterministic shortlists. Many weaker variants still recompute full HVs repeatedly, use Python-level loops and vstacking frequently, or lack an HV-call budget.
- Data reuse patterns matter: precomputing ref - pts, reusing product-of-diffs, and storing hv_single/pair_hv avoid recomputation. Several heuristics still rebuild arrays or recompute trivial proxies inside hot loops.
- Determinism and tie-breaking are handled consistently in stronger heuristics (fixed RNG seeds, lexsort tie-breaks). Some weaker ones use random.shuffle or non-seeded RNG patterns that reduce reproducibility.
- Pruning and upper bounds: effective ones use box-volume or singleton HV as cheap upper bounds to skip expensive evaluations; weaker designs often miss these prunes and evaluate every candidate.
- Local search: bounded, deterministic 1-for-1 swaps with early exit are useful; exhaustive GSI-like full recomputation (remove-each candidate) is expensive and less scalable.
- Implementation-level inefficiencies to avoid: frequent Python membership tests on lists (use sets), repeated np.vstack in loops, recomputing the same masks, and computing pairwise HVs beyond a small threshold.
experience: Always budget and minimize HV calls: prioritize vectorized proxies for aggressive pruning, cache singleton HVs, compute pairwise HVs only for very small shortlists, and use CELF lazy timestamps to amortize updates. Favor deterministic, O(N log N) shortlist construction and bounded, incremental swaps. Optimize hot loops with numpy and sets to avoid Python overhead; add an explicit HV-call budget and conservative upper bounds to maximize runtime-quality trade-offs.
current_reflection: Always budget and minimize HV calls: prioritize vectorized proxies for aggressive pruning, cache singleton HVs, compute pairwise HVs only for very small shortlists, and use CELF lazy timestamps to amortize updates. Favor deterministic, O(N log N) shortlist construction and bounded, incremental swaps. Optimize hot loops with numpy and sets to avoid Python overhead; add an explicit HV-call budget and conservative upper bounds to maximize runtime-quality trade-offs.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.
bad_reflections: Prefer small adaptive shortlists (O(k) with Pareto awareness), use CELF with robust singleton/pairwise caches and box‑vol upper bounds, cap HV calls, vectorize dominance/proxy computations, avoid Python-level list removals in hot loops, and evaluate swaps via marginal deltas or cheap pruning before full HV calls. Exploit low‑D exact HV routines when available and keep deterministic tie‑breaking.

Favor deterministic Pareto prefilter, vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, precompute singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps, conditionally cache pairs for small shortlists, apply tight cheap upper bounds to prune, cap HV calls, and use efficient numpy masks/sets. These deliver high-quality hypervolume with far fewer expensive HV evaluations.

For the next generation, combine: adaptive shortlist sizing (scale with pareto size and k), CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, and tight upper-bound pruning (box-vol or scalar HVC) to avoid useless HV calls. Add an HV-call budget, incremental HV updates, and prioritize swap candidates via proxy gains or diversity. Vectorize dominance checks and weight computations; keep all randomness seeded and tie-break deterministically.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: hypervolume, HV budget, CELF, singleton cache, vectorized proxies, shortlist, deterministic, upper bounds, incremental swaps, numpy
- Advice: Build a deterministic O(N log N) shortlist with vectorized dominance/proxy screening, cache singleton HVCs, use CELF lazy timestamps, cap HV calls and swap iterations, enable pairwise caches only for tiny shortlists, apply cheap box/volume upper bounds, and optimize hot loops with numpy/sets
- Avoid: Unbounded HV calls, nested HV recomputations, Python-level hot-loop removals, non-deterministic tie-breaking, and large pairwise caches
- Explanation: Budgeting + caching + vectorized pruning yields maximal HV-quality per expensive exact HV evaluation while keeping runtime predictable and reproducible
